386: Preface: The Contradictions of Theory
387: 
388: This book is an introductory essay on archaeological theory. It tries to
389: explain something of what ‘theory’ is, its relationship to
390: archaeological practice, how it has developed within archaeology over
391: the last few decades, and how archaeological thought relates to theory
392: in the human sciences and the intellectual world generally.
393: 
394: To many, ‘theory’ is a dirty word both within and outside archaeology.
395: Prince Charles earned almost universal approbation when he condemned
396: ‘trendy theorists’ in education; nobody however, including the Prince
397: himself, seemed to be very clear precisely who he meant. When visiting
398: an archaeological site a few years ago a suggestion of mine met with
399: laughter and the response ‘that’s a typical suggestion of a theorist’. I
400: don’t recall anyone telling me exactly why my suggestion was so absurd,
401: and when I visited the site the following year the strategy had been
402: adopted. For the meat-and-potatoes Anglo-Saxon world in particular,
403: theory is an object of profound suspicion. It is a popular saying that
404: for the English, to be called an intellectual is to be suspected of
405: wanting to steal someone’s wife (sexism in the original). Theory,
406: ‘political correctness’ and being ‘foreign’ stand together in the dock
407: as traits to be regarded with hostility in the Englishspeaking world–and
408: beyond; there is even a word for hostility to theory in
409: German–Theorifeindlichkeit. I shall look at some of the reasons why this
410: is so in chapter 1.
411: 
412: At the same time, however, theory is increasingly popular, and seen as
413: increasingly important, both within and outside archaeology. Valentine
414: Cunningham commented in The Times Higher Education Supplement that
415: theorists in academia are ‘a surging band, cocky, confident in academic
416: credentials, job security and intellectual prestige’, inspiring the
417: columnist Laurie Taylor to write a memorable account of a bunch of
418: theorists intellectually roughing up a more empirical colleague at a
419: seminar before departing to the local bar. His account was fictitious
420: but contained much truth.
421: 
422: ‘You’re a terrorist? Thank God. I understood Meg to say you were a
423: theorist.’ From Culler (1997: 16)
424: 
425: [fpref_image001.jpg]
426: 
427: There are various indices of the ‘success’ of an explicitly defined
428: archaeological theory; one might cite the frequency of ‘theoretical’
429: symposia at major conferences such as the Society for American
430: Archaeology or the European Association of Archaeology, or the incidence
431: of ‘theory’ articles in the major journals. One particularly telling
432: index is the rise and rise of the British Theoretical Archaeology Group
433: conference (TAG). This was formed as a small talking-shop for British
434: archaeological theorists in the late 1970s, but since then has become
435: the largest annual archaeological conference in Britain with substantial
436: participation from North America and Europe. There are now similar
437: organizations in North America, Scandinavia (NordicTAG) and Germany
438: (Arbeitsgemeinschaft Theorie).
439: 
440: It is true that a lot of papers delivered at TAG scarcely merit the term
441: ‘theoretical’, and even more true that many only come for the infamous
442: TAG party in any case. It must also be conceded that the degree of
443: impact of TAGs and ‘theory’s’ influence on the ‘real world’ of
444: archaeological practice, and the cultural and legislative framework of
445: archaeology, is debatable. The theorist often feels like Cassandra,
446: constantly giving what he or she sees as profound predictions and
447: insight and constantly being ignored by the decision makers.
448: 
449: This book is written to give the student an introduction to a few of the
450: strands of current thinking in archaeological theory. It is deliberately
451: written as an introduction, in as clear and jargon-free a fashion as the
452: author can manage (though as we shall see, criteria of clarity and of
453: what constitutes jargon are riddled with problems).
454: 
455: It is intended as a ‘route map’ for the student. That is, it seeks to
456: point out prominent landmarks on the terrain of theory, comment on
457: relationships between different bodies of thought, and to clarify the
458: intellectual underpinnings of certain views. As such, it is anything but
459: an encyclopaedia; it is hardly one-tenth of a comprehensive guide to the
460: field, if such a guide could be written. The text should be read with
461: reference to the Further Reading and Glossary sections, and
462: over-generalization, oversimplification and caricatures of viewpoints
463: are necessary evils.
464: 
465: Above all, I remind all readers of the fourth word in the title of this
466: book. I have tried to write an Introduction. The book, and its different
467: chapters, are meant to be a starting-point for the student on a range of
468: issues, which she or he can then explore in greater depth through the
469: Further Reading sections. Many of the comments and criticisms made of
470: the first edition of this book focused on an alleged over-or
471: under-emphasis of a particular theoretical viewpoint, or lack of
472: coverage. Many of these criticisms were valid, and I have tried to deal
473: with them in this second edition; but many evaluated the text as a
474: position statement with which they happened to agree or disagree, rather
475: than on its pedagogical intention, that is as an introductory route-map
476: to the issues. Additionally, students need to be reminded that this book
477: should be the start, not the end, of their reading and thinking, a point
478: I will return to in the Conclusion. A route-map is not an encyclopaedia.
479: 
480: To pursue the route map analogy, the route followed here is one of
481: several that could be taken through the terrain of archaeological
482: theory. I could have devoted a chapter each to different thematic areas:
483: Landscape, The Household, Trade and Exchange, Cultures and Style,
484: Agency, and so on. In each case, a variety of approaches to that theme
485: could be given to show how different theories contradict or complement
486: each other and produce different sorts of explanation of the
487: archaeological record. Alternatively, a tour could be taken through
488: different ‘isms’: positivism, functionalism, Marxism, structuralism,
489: poststructuralism, feminism. These would be reasonable paths, and ones
490: moreover that have been taken by other authors.
491: 
492: This book, however, tries above all to bring out the relationship
493: between archaeological thought and wider strands of theory in
494: intellectual and cultural life as a whole. It seeks to show how specific
495: theoretical positions taken by individual archaeologists ‘make sense’
496: within a wider context, cultural, social and political as well as
497: academic. This book also seeks to bring out the relationship between
498: archaeological theory and archaeological practice more clearly than has
499: been done in the past. The structure adopted here, of a historical
500: approach focusing initially on the New Archaeology and reactions to it
501: before moving on to current debates, fitted this purpose best.
502: 
503: I have written above that this book is a guide for ‘the student’; I mean
504: the student in the broadest sense. Many practising archaeologists
505: employed outside the academic world have told me that they are
506: interested in current theoretical debates, and see such debates as of
507: potential relevance to their work. Nevertheless many feel alienated by
508: what they see as the unnecessary obscurity and pretentiousness that is
509: central to the theoretical scene. I don’t subscribe to such an analysis,
510: but I have to acknowledge that it is widespread. Right or wrong, I hope
511: that they may find that what follows is of some help.
512: 
513: In trying to survey many different theoretical strands, I have been torn
514: between trying to write a ‘neutral’, ‘objective’ survey of different
515: currents of thought on the one hand, and a committed polemic advancing
516: my own views on the other. The end product lies, perhaps a little
517: unhappily, somewhere between these extremes. On the one hand, the
518: construction of a completely objective survey simply isn’t
519: intellectually possible; the most biased and partial views on any
520: academic subject consistently come from those who overtly proclaim that
521: their own position is neutral, detached and valuefree. In addition, it
522: would be disingenuous to claim that the book is written from a
523: disinterested viewpoint–that it is a guide pure and simple. Obviously an
524: interest in theory goes hand-in-hand with a passionate belief in its
525: importance, and an attachment to certain more or less controversial
526: views within the field.
527: 
528: On the other hand, if we want to understand why theory is where it is
529: today, any account of a wide diversity of intellectual positions must
530: endeavour to be reasonably sympathetic to all parties. A survey can
531: never be neutral, but it can make some attempt to be fair. As R.G.
532: Collingwood pointed out in relation to the history of philosophy, most
533: theoretical positions arise out of the perceived importance of certain
534: contexts or issues; that is, philosophical beliefs are in part responses
535: to particular sets of problems, and have to be understood as such rather
536: than given an intellectual mugging. One’s intellectual opponents are
537: never all morons or charlatans to the last man and woman and one’s
538: bedfellows are rarely all exciting, first-rate scholars. Before we get
539: carried away with such piety it must be remembered that this does not
540: mean that certain positions are not therefore immune from criticism. An
541: intellectual relativism in which ‘all viewpoints are equally valid’ or
542: in which ‘every theory is possible’ is not a rigorous or tenable
543: position. We can see historically that some theoretical positions have
544: been abandoned as dead ends, for example the extreme logical positivism
545: of the 1970s.
546: 
547: I have also been torn between writing a historical account of the
548: development of theory, and of giving a ‘snapshot’ of theory in the
549: present. On the one hand, it might be held that my re-telling of the
550: origins of the New Archaeology of the 1960s, and more arguably the
551: processual/postprocessual ‘wars’ of the 1980s and 1990s, is now out of
552: date. On the other hand, I feel that in order for the student to
553: understand where theory is today, it is necessary to look at its
554: development over the last few decades, and indeed to look at the deeper
555: intellectual roots of many views and positions in the more remote past,
556: for example in the thinking of figures like Charles Darwin and Karl
557: Marx. Much of traditional cultural evolutionary theory, and much of the
558: early postprocessual critique, may appear to be passé to some; but I do
559: not think that the modern student can understand current thinking
560: without reference back to this literature. Archaeology would be a
561: strange field of study if it asserted that it could understand the way
562: the discipline thinks in the present, without reference back to the way
563: it thought in the past.
564: 
565: For this second edition of the book, I have made a number of changes. I
566: give a more extended account of these and reflection on them in the
567: Further Reading section, but two stand out. First, there has been an
568: explosion in archaeological discussion of Darwinian evolution, and I
569: have therefore divided the chapter on ‘Evolution’ into two. Second, the
570: first edition concentrated on theory in the Anglo-American world, in
571: part reflecting my own background and limitations. This concentration
572: was rightly criticized by many non-Anglo scholars. I have tried in this
573: second edition to write a more inclusive text, giving more attention to
574: Indigenous and postcolonial perspectives as well as drawing more
575: attention to theoretical contributions from across the world. I have
576: nevertheless retained the original structure of the book, and have run
577: the risk of ‘fitting in’ material around this organizing structure; but
578: the alternatives, for example of having a separate chapter on non-Anglo
579: theory, or of a country-by-country survey, seemed to me to be greater
580: evils and to do greater violence to the very subtle texture of
581: theoretical debate.
582: 
583: The adoption of an informal tone and omission of detailed referencing
584: from the text is deliberate. It is to help, I hope, the clarity of its
585: arguments and the ease with which it can be read. Many ‘academic’
586: writers have often been taught to forsake the use of the ‘I’ word, to
587: attempt to render our writing neutral and distant, to avoid a
588: conversational or informal tone, all in the name of scientific or
589: scholarly detachment. This may or may not be a valid project. The aim
590: here however is educational rather than scholarly in the narrow sense.
591: 
592: One of my central points, particularly in the first chapter, is that all
593: practising archaeologists use theory whether they like it or not. To
594: make this point clear and to furnish examples I have often quoted
595: passages from avowedly ‘atheoretical’ writers and commented upon them,
596: to draw out the theories and assumptions that lie implicit within those
597: passages. In most cases the passages come from the first suitable book
598: to hand. I want to stress that critiques of these examples are not
599: personal attacks on the writers concerned. Here, the need to use
600: practical examples to make a theoretical point clear clashes with the
601: desire to avoid a perception of unfair, personalized criticism.
602: 
603: The text is based in part on lecture notes for various undergraduate
604: courses I have taught at Sheffield, Lampeter, Durham and Southampton.
605: The students at all four institutions are thanked for their constructive
606: and helpful responses. Some students may recognize themselves in the
607: dialogues in some of the chapters, and I ask their forgiveness for this.
608: The first edition of the book was partly conceived while I was a
609: Research Fellow at the University of California at Berkeley in the
610: spring of 1995. I would like to thank Meg Conkey, Christine Hastorf,
611: Marcia Ann Dobres, Margot Winer and many others too numerous to mention
612: for their hospitality during that time and for making my stay so
613: enjoyable and profitable. I also thank Durham University for giving me
614: study leave for that term. A number of reviewers, some anonymous, made a
615: string of invaluable comments without which the book would have been
616: much more opinionated and parochial and much less comprehensible. These
617: include especially Randy McGuire, Jim Hill, Chris Tilley and Elizabeth
618: Brumfiel. Robert Preucel and Ian Hodder reviewed the final draft
619: extensively. Tim Earle, Clive Gamble and Cynthia Robin kindly corrected
620: my misconceptions for the second edition. Dominic McNamara drew my
621: attention to the Foucault quotation in chapter 6. Within the Department
622: of Archaeology at Durham, Helena Hamerow, Colin Haselgrove, Anthony
623: Harding, Simon James, Sam Lucy and Martin Millett read and made
624: invaluable comments on the first draft. Brian Boyd, Zoe Crossland, Jim
625: Brown and John McNabb helped with illustrations. William R. Iseminger
626: kindly supplied figure 9.5 and Francis Wenban-Smith kindly supplied
627: figure 10.4. Collaboration with staff of the History, Classics and
628: Archaeology Subject Centre, particularly Annie Grant, Tom Dowson and
629: Anthony Sinclair, influenced my thinking on the pedagogical framing and
630: impact of the second edition. Conversations on the philosophy of science
631: with my late father C. David Johnson clarified many points.
632: 
633: I moved to Southampton in 2004; I thank an outstanding group of
634: colleagues and students there for their advice and support over the last
635: five years. I prepared revisions for the second edition while a visiting
636: scholar at the University of Pennsylvania in the autumn of 2008. I thank
637: Bob Preucel and Richard Hodges for making that visit possible, and the
638: students of Bob’s theory class for their input and hospitality. I also
639: thank Clare Smith, Heather Burke and Matt Spriggs for organizing a
640: stimulating visit to Australia in 2003/4, and Prof Joseph Maran, Ulrich
641: Thaler and the staff and students of Heidelberg University for four
642: wonderful months discussing theory and practice in spring 2005.
643: 
644: More broadly, can I thank everyone who has taken the time to speak or
645: write to me over the last ten years to express their appreciation for
646: this book. Students, teachers and practicing archaeologists have given
647: me some very kind compliments, for which I am flattered and grateful,
648: from the Berkeley feminist who said that I wrote like a woman to the
649: Flinders student who sent me a picture of her Matthew Johnson Theory
650: Action Doll.
651: 
652: Conversations with Chris Taylor, Paul Everson, Casper Johnson and David
653: Stocker informed the discussion of Bodiam in chapter 10, though errors
654: and misconceptions in this discussion remain my responsibility. John
655: Davey, Tessa Harvey, Jane Huber, Rosalie Robertson and Julia Kirk at
656: Blackwell were always patient, encouraging and ready with practical help
657: when needed. My wife Becky made comments on successive drafts; proofread
658: the final manuscript; and most importantly, provided emotional and
659: intellectual support without which this book would never have been
660: written. In return, I hope this book explains to her why archaeologists
661: are such a peculiar bunch of human beings, though I know she has her own
662: theories in this respect. My thanks to everybody.
663: 
664: 1
665: 
666: Common Sense is Not Enough
667: 
668: Archaeology can be very boring, distressing and physically
669: uncomfortable. Every year we excavate thousands of sites, some with
670: painstaking and mind-numbing patience, some in a great and undignified
671: hurry. Every year we get chilled to the marrow or bitten half to death
672: by mosquitoes while visiting some unprepossessing, grassy mound in the
673: middle of nowhere. Miles from a decent restaurant or even a warm bath,
674: we try to look interested while the rain comes down in sheets and some
675: great professor whose best work was 20 years ago witters on in a
676: monotone about what was found in Trench 4B. Every year we churn out
677: thousands of interminable, stultifyingly dull site reports, fretting
678: over the accuracy of plans and diagrams, collating lists of grubby
679: artefacts to publish that few will ever consult or use again.
680: 
681: Why?
682: 
683: We could spend the money on hospitals. Alternatively we could quietly
684: pocket the cash and write a much more entertaining, fictitious version
685: of what the past was like while we sat on a sun-kissed terrace somewhere
686: in southern California. If we were feeling ideologically sound we could
687: raise an International Brigade for a liberation struggle somewhere. Each
688: of these alternatives has its attractions, but we don’t do any of these
689: things. We go on as we have done before.
690: 
691: One reason we don’t do these things is because archaeology is very
692: important. The past is dead and gone, but it is also very powerful. It
693: is so powerful that an entire nation (Zimbabwe) can name itself after an
694: archaeological site. It is so powerful that archaeological sites are
695: surrounded by police and are the subject of attempted occupations by New
696: Age travellers. It is so powerful that even individual groups of
697: artefacts like the Parthenon frieze are the subject of major
698: international disputes.
699: 
700: The question ‘why do we do archaeology?’ is therefore bound up with the
701: question ‘why is archaeology – the study of the past through its
702: material remains – so important to us?’ And this again leads on to the
703: question of ‘us’, of our identity – who are we? And these are all
704: theoretical questions.
705: 
706: Definitions of Theory
707: 
708: ‘Theory’ is a very difficult word to define. Indeed, I shall return to
709: this topic in the final chapter, since different theoretical views
710: define ‘theory’ in different ways. Different definitions cannot
711: therefore be fully explored without prior explanation of those views.
712: 
713: For the time being, I propose to define theory as follows: theory is the
714: order we put facts in. I will go on to discuss the extent to which
715: ‘facts’ exist independently of theory, and how we might define ‘facts’.
716: We can also note that most archaeologists would include within the
717: purview of theory why we do archaeology and the social and cultural
718: context of archaeology. They would also refer to issues of
719: interpretation. Most archaeologists would agree that the way we
720: interpret the past has ‘theoretical’ aspects in the broad sense. For
721: example, we could cite general theories such as cultural and biological
722: evolution, issues of how we go about testing our ideas, debates over how
723: we should think about stylistic or decorative change in artefacts.
724: 
725: There is disagreement over whether many concepts can be considered
726: ‘theoretical’ or whether they are merely neutral techniques or methods
727: outside the purview of theory. Stratigraphy, excavation and recording
728: techniques, and the use of statistical methods are, for example, clearly
729: all examples of putting facts in a certain order. However, they might be
730: considered ‘theoretical’ by some but ‘just practical’ or ‘simply
731: techniques’ by others. Theory and method are often confused by
732: archaeologists. In this more restricted sense of theory, if theory
733: covers the ‘why’ questions, method or methodology covers the ‘how’
734: questions. So theory covers why we selected this site to dig, method how
735: we dig it. However, theory and method are obviously closely related, and
736: many archaeologists including myself regard such a straightforward
737: division as too simple.
738: 
739: To give an example of the relationship between theory and method, we
740: might consider different methods of investigating social inequality in
741: the archaeological record. Thus the method archaeologists might use
742: would be to compare graves ‘richly’ endowed with lots of grave goods
743: with poorer, unadorned graves. It is evident in this exercise that
744: certain ideas or theories about the nature of social inequality are
745: being assumed (that social status will be reflected in treatment of the
746: body at death, that material goods are unequally distributed through
747: society and that this has a direct relationship to social inequality,
748: and so on). These ideas are themselves theoretical in nature.
749: 
750: Perhaps theory and method are one and the same thing and cannot be
751: separated; perhaps they have to be separated if archaeology is to be a
752: rigorous discipline that is capable of testing its theories against its
753: data. This is a debate we shall return to in chapter 4.
754: 
755: I’m sorry to butt in, but all this discussion of theory and method
756: clearly demonstrates just how sterile and boring theory really is.
757: You’re already lost in definitions and semantics, you haven’t mentioned
758: a single fact about the past, and I’m beginning to wish I hadn’t
759: bothered to start reading this and had turned my attention to that new
760: book about the Hopewell culture instead. Theory is irrelevant to the
761: practice of archaeology; we can just use our common sense.
762: 
763: Ah, Roger, the eternal empiricist. (Roger Beefy is an undergraduate
764: student at Northern University, England, though women and men like Roger
765: can be found in any archaeological institution. Roger fell in love with
766: archaeology when he was a child, scrambling up and down the ruins of
767: local castles, churches, burial mounds and other sites. Roger spent a
768: year after school before coming to Northern University digging and
769: working in museums. Roger loves handling archaeological material, and is
770: happiest when drawing a section or talking about seriation techniques
771: over a beer. Now, in his second year at Northern University, Roger has
772: found himself in the middle of a compulsory ‘theory course’. Full of
773: twaddle about middle-range theory, hermeneutics and postcoloniality, it
774: seems to have nothing to do with the subject he loves.)
775: 
776: So, you want to know why theory is ‘relevant’ to archaeological
777: practice. Perhaps you will bear with me while I discuss four possible
778: reasons.
779: 
780: 1 We need to justify what we do
781: 
782: Our audience (other archaeologists, people in other disciplines, the
783: ‘general public’ or ‘community’ however defined) needs to have a clear
784: idea from archaeologists of why our research is important, why it is
785: worth paying for, why we are worth listening to. There are a hundred
786: possible answers to this challenge of justification, for example:
787: 
788: -   The past is intrinsically important, and we need to find out about
789:     it for its own sake.
790: -   We need to know where we came from to know where we’re going next.
791:     Knowledge of the past leads to better judgements about the future.
792: -   Only archaeology has the time depth of many thousands of years
793:     needed to generate comparative observations about long-term culture
794:     processes.
795: -   Archaeology is one medium of cultural revolution that will
796:     emancipate ordinary people from repressive ideologies.
797: 
798: The chances are that you disagree with at least one of these statements,
799: and agree with at least one other. That doesn’t change the fact that
800: each statement is a theoretical proposition that needs justifying,
801: arguing through, and debating before it can be accepted or rejected.
802: None of the statements given above is obvious, self-evident or
803: common-sensical when examined closely. Indeed, very little in the world
804: is obvious or self-evident when examined closely, though our political
805: leaders would have us think otherwise.
806: 
807: 2 We need to evaluate one interpretation of the past against another, to
808: decide which is the stronger
809: 
810: Archaeology relies in part for its intellectual credibility on being
811: able to distinguish ‘good’ from ‘bad’ interpretations of the past. Were
812: the people who lived on this site hunter-gatherers, or were they aliens
813: from the planet Zog? Which is the stronger interpretation?
814: 
815: It’s impossible to decide what is a strong archaeological interpretation
816: on the basis of ‘common sense’ alone. Common sense might suggest, for
817: example, that we accept the explanation that covers the greatest number
818: of facts. There may be thousands of sherds of pottery dating from the
819: first millennium BC on a site, all factual in their own way, but one
820: other fact – a treering date of ad 750, for example – may suggest that
821: they might be all ‘residual’ or left over from an earlier period. In
822: practice, every day of our working lives as archaeologists, we decide on
823: which order to put our facts in, what degree of importance to place on
824: different pieces of evidence. When we do this, we use theoretical
825: criteria to decide which facts are important and which are not worth
826: bothering with.
827: 
828: A good example of the inadequacy of common sense in deciding what is a
829: strong or weak archaeological explanation is that of ley lines. Ley
830: lines were ‘discovered’ by Alfred Watkins in the 1920s, when he noticed
831: that many ancient archaeological sites in Britain could be linked up by
832: straight lines. The idea that ancient sites lay on straight lines could
833: be ‘proved’ easily by taking a map upon which such ancient monuments
834: were marked and drawing such lines through them. Watkins suggested these
835: lines represented prehistoric trackways. Nonsense, said the professional
836: archaeological community. It was common sense that prehistoric peoples
837: living thousands of years before literacy or formal geometry were far
838: too primitive to lay out such geometrically sophisticated lines. Watkins
839: had intended his book as a genuine contribution to archaeology, but his
840: research, sincerely carried out, was laughed out of court and consigned
841: to the ranks of lunatic ‘fringe archaeology’. Other writers took his
842: thesis up in succeeding decades but extended it by suggesting that the
843: lines were of sacred significance or mystical power.
844: 
845: Now it is quite clear today that prehistoric peoples would have been
846: quite capable of laying out such lines. The original, common-sensical
847: criteria used by archaeologists for rejecting Watkins’s thesis were
848: completely invalid.
849: 
850: Ley lines do not exist. This was shown by Tom Williamson and Liz Bellamy
851: in Ley Lines in Question, which analysed such lines statistically and
852: showed that the density of archaeological sites in the British landscape
853: is so great that a line drawn through virtually anywhere will ‘clip’ a
854: number of sites. It took Williamson and Bellamy a book’s worth of effort
855: and statistical sophistication to prove this, however.
856: 
857: The moral of the debate over ley lines is that what is considered to
858: constitute a strong or a weak explanation is not simply a matter of
859: ‘common sense’. I would argue that if we really want to understand what
860: drove and continues to drive the ley line debate, we have to look, in
861: part, at class divides in British archaeology. In his time Watkins was
862: derided as a vulgar amateur, while today the tradition of ley line
863: searchers continues strongly in ‘alternative’ or New Age circles. New
864: Age travellers and others in their turn view middle-class professional
865: archaeologists with suspicion. Others might dispute this social
866: interpretation and suggest alternative reasons for the intellectual
867: development of the issue. I might reply: we would then be having a
868: theoretical debate.
869: 
870: 3 We must be explicit in what we do as archaeologists
871: 
872: In other words, we must be as open as possible about our reasons,
873: approaches and biases, rather than trying to conceal them or pretend
874: that they do not exist. This is a basic rule of academic discourse,
875: though it is not always followed. Lewis Binford, a character we shall
876: meet properly in the next chapter, made the point that all scientists of
877: all disciplines need to be aware of the assumptions they are making if
878: they wish to be productive.
879: 
880: It goes without saying that we can never be completely explicit about
881: our biases and preconceptions. This should not stop us trying.
882: 
883: 4 We don’t ‘need’ theory, we all use theory whether we like it or not
884: 
885: Put another way, we are all theorists. This is the most important point
886: of all. The most lowly troweller, the most bored washer of ceramics, the
887: most alienated finds assistant or lab technician, are all theoreticians
888: in the sense that they all use theories, concepts, ideas, assumptions in
889: their work. (The theory may have been imposed on them by the project
890: director or funding body, but it is theory nevertheless.) Put another
891: way, the driest, most descriptive text or site report is already
892: theoretical. Somebody wielding a WHS or Marshalltown trowel relies on
893: theories of soil colour change and stratigraphy in his or her work;
894: editorial judgements about the relative weighting and order given to
895: pottery and artefact reports in a site monograph depend on a judgement
896: on what is ‘significant’ about that particular site which in turn rests
897: on theoretical criteria.
898: 
899: Any archaeologist who therefore tells you that their work is
900: ‘atheoretical’, that they are ‘not interested in theory’, or that they
901: are doing ‘real archaeology’ as opposed to those ‘trendy theorists’ is
902: not telling the whole truth. They are as much theorists as anyone else,
903: though they might choose to mask their theoretical preconceptions by
904: labelling them ‘common sense’ or derived from the ‘real world’. In doing
905: so, I would argue that they are bypassing their responsibility to make
906: clear the intellectual basis of their work, trying to hide the
907: theoretical assumptions and approaches that they are in fact using from
908: critical scrutiny. They are indulging in an intellectual sleight of
909: hand.
910: 
911: I would go further: pretending to be atheoretical is an attempt to
912: impose a kind of machismo on to archaeological practice. As we shall see
913: in chapter 8, archaeological practice is bound up with gendered notions
914: of what is or is not valuable. There is, at least in the
915: English-speaking world, always something vaguely effeminate (and
916: therefore, it is implied, somehow secondary) about talking, reasoning,
917: discussing, trying to think clearly and explicitly. It is difficult to
918: see Vin Diesel at a philosophy discussion group. ‘Real men’ don’t do
919: isms and ologies; they just dig – preferably with a really large, heavy
920: pickaxe.
921: 
922: I’ve listened long enough to this; you’re descending into abuse now. I’m
923: willing to concede that we all use theory in some sense, but at the end
924: of the day it’s the facts, the raw data, that count.
925: 
926: I’m not going to argue now about whether ‘raw data’ really exist
927: independently of theory – that will come later. Let’s suppose for now
928: that raw data really do exist. Where does that get us? There is an
929: infinity of archaeological facts. They are piled in their millions in
930: museum and laboratory storerooms, in microfiche lists and in tables of
931: data. Here are some pretty undeniable ‘facts’:
932: 
933: The pot I am holding is 600 years old.
934: 
935: Cuzco is an Inca site in Peru.
936: 
937: Lepenski Vir is a Mesolithic site in Serbia.
938: 
939: Colono Ware pottery has been found in Virginia.
940: 
941: A skeleton was excavated at Maiden Castle, Dorset, England, with an iron
942: projectile lodged in its spine.
943: 
944: Great Basin projectile points come in different sizes.
945: 
946: The Bronze Age preceded the Iron Age.
947: 
948: Tikal was a major ceremonial centre for the Ancient Maya.
949: 
950: There are usually lots of clay pipe fragments on post-1500 sites. The
951: Dordogne area of France is full of cave art.
952: 
953: The Great Wall of China is studded with towers.
954: 
955: In Chaco Canyon the ancient pueblos are built of stone.
956: 
957: Do the sentences above add up to a meaningful account of the past, a
958: coherent archaeological narrative? No. Simply dredging up facts and
959: waiting for them to cohere into an orderly account of the past is like
960: putting a number of monkeys in front of typewriters and waiting for them
961: to come up with the complete works of Shakespeare.
962: 
963: What makes us archaeologists as opposed to mindless collectors of old
964: junk is the set of rules we use to translate those facts into meaningful
965: accounts of the past, accounts that ‘make sense’ to us as archaeologists
966: and (it is hoped) to those who read or engage with our work. And those
967: rules, whether they are implicit or explicit, are theoretical in nature.
968: Facts are important, but without theory they remain utterly silent.
969: 
970: Let’s take the example of a distinguished Professor of Archaeology who
971: claims to be writing in an atheoretical, factual manner using ‘common
972: sense’, and see what he is really doing. I have selected this text more
973: or less at random:
974: 
975: It is worth stressing that Romano-British culture was based on a money
976: economy. In south-eastern Britain coins were indeed in use before the
977: conquest, but the Romans were responsible for spreading their
978: circulation throughout the island. The extent to which currency
979: permeated the whole commercial life of the country, down to the smallest
980: transactions, may be gauged from the occurrence of coins on the humblest
981: Romano-British sites and in the remotest part of the province. (Alcock
982: 1976: 174)
983: 
984: One theoretical assumption being made here is that ideas like
985: ‘transaction’ and ‘commercial life’, which only gain their modern
986: meaning in the later eighteenth century and only arguably so even then,
987: can easily be applied to Roman Britain without further explication. It
988: follows that the writer must expect the reader to use his or her modern
989: experience of transactions and commercial life – market oriented,
990: largely unconnected with social relations, mediated by a common means of
991: monetary exchange – to understand the meaning of the sentence. This and
992: other assumptions may or may not be true, but they are theoretical in
993: nature.
994: 
995: A second is a ‘middle-range’ assumption: that is, it connects particular
996: facts on the one hand to general theories on the other (see chapter 4).
997: Alcock assumes that the relative numbers of coins on different site
998: types (note the use of an implicit site hierarchy that equates with a
999: social hierarchy, assumed rather than demonstrated: ‘the humblest
1000: sites’) will accurately reflect the level of what Alcock has termed
1001: ‘commercial activity’. Of course, we have already acknowledged that
1002: commercial activity is a much more theoretically complex beast. Again,
1003: this is a theoretical proposition.
1004: 
1005: Alcock’s account may or may not be ‘true’, a ‘fair picture’ or ‘valid’;
1006: that is a matter for debate among those specializing in this period. It
1007: is certainly deeply theoretical. I could go on analysing the passage for
1008: several more pages, but the point has been made that even the most
1009: apparently straightforward, transparent, ‘clear’ prose conceals
1010: theoretical depths.
1011: 
1012: All this is very plausible and convincing, but I still dislike theory
1013: intensely. Theorists seem constantly to use incomprehensible jargon,
1014: write in an impenetrable style, and never to get anywhere tangible. You
1015: might persuade me there is a point to theory, but you can’t stop me
1016: being irritated and alienated by what theorists write.
1017: 
1018: No, I can’t. I get irritated by a lot of theoretical writing, just as I
1019: get irritated by all sorts of archaeological writing. But you’ve raised
1020: a lot of points here that are worth taking in turn.
1021: 
1022: First, why the ‘jargon’? Long words with specialized meanings are not
1023: confined to archaeological theory. Every area within archaeology has its
1024: own specialist terms of reference; in this sense jargon is in the eye of
1025: the beholder. My familiar terms as a theorist or as a specialist in
1026: vernacular architecture may seem jargon to the environmental specialist,
1027: and those of the environmental specialist may equally seem jargon to me.
1028: 
1029: There is a deeper problem with the accusation of jargon, however. There
1030: seems to be an assumption behind such an accusation that we can always
1031: express what we want to say in ‘clear, simple and easy’ language. If
1032: only archaeology were so straightforward! If it were, we might have
1033: concluded the archaeological project with a perfect understanding of the
1034: past hundreds of years ago. Archaeology is, if nothing else, about new
1035: ideas about the past. We express ideas in words, and it may be
1036: appropriate to use new words to lead the reader to think in new ways.
1037: 
1038: Human societies were and are very complex things. As part of the natural
1039: world they share its complexity, and also have a social and cultural
1040: complexity all of their own. We don’t complain when the chemist or
1041: biologist uses technical language incomprehensible to the lay person, so
1042: why should we when the archaeologist does so?
1043: 
1044: The point I am making here is that archaeologists expect the finer
1045: techniques of archaeological practice to be difficult to comprehend and
1046: master; that is the nature of our discipline. We are prepared to put
1047: effort into mastering the language and practice of stratigraphy, Harris
1048: matrices, seriation, scientific dating techniques, even the
1049: half-intuitive practical skill of differentiating between layers by the
1050: feel of the soil under the trowel. But the ‘theory’ side of what we do –
1051: using the tiny scraps of information thus gained to tell us about the
1052: human past in all its richness and complexity – must be at least equally
1053: difficult as these ‘practical’ tasks. It fact, it must be one of the
1054: most intellectually demanding tasks we as a species have ever set
1055: ourselves.
1056: 
1057: I think you’re missing the point. The suspicion is that jargon is being
1058: used to mystify, to create a language of exclusion where the outsider is
1059: made to feel small.
1060: 
1061: There is some justice in this charge. Certain forms of academic rhetoric
1062: are used, intentionally or unintentionally, to set up in-groups and
1063: out-groups. I do not defend such a practice. But again, one hears the
1064: vague murmur of pots calling kettles black; all sectional interests
1065: within and outside archaeology do this. Read any article in Vernacular
1066: Architecture on the classification of scarf-joints with squinted and
1067: pegged abutments, or a medieval historian on enfeoffments and
1068: subinfeudation.
1069: 
1070: Finally, ‘writing clearly’ assumes that one is writing about something
1071: else. In other words, that there is a real, external world out there
1072: with certain essential, concrete features, features that language can
1073: describe in a more or less clear and neutral manner. Now whether one is
1074: describing the decoration on pots or suggesting what it might have been
1075: like to live in the Bronze Age, this is a highly debatable assumption.
1076: Certainly, in most traditions of Western thought, the past doesn’t exist
1077: anywhere outside our own heads. I have never touched, kicked or felt the
1078: past.
1079: 
1080: Theory is difficult. If one accepts that all archaeologists are
1081: theorists, then logically it is no more or less difficult than any other
1082: branch of archaeology. But archaeology itself is difficult. We have set
1083: ourselves an incredibly daunting task. We want to understand human
1084: societies that have been dead and gone for thousands of years, whose
1085: customs, values and attitudes were almost certainly utterly different
1086: from our own. We have to do this without talking to the people
1087: themselves. What is more, we want to understand how and why they changed
1088: in the way they did. And the only materials we have to achieve this
1089: immense task are a few paltry scraps of rubbish they left behind on the
1090: way, most of which have long since decayed into dust. Such a task is not
1091: a simple one; the wish that, for all its practical discomforts and
1092: difficulties, it be an intellectually easy one is quite understandable,
1093: but very naïve.
1094: 
1095: Theory is also difficult for reasons that have less to do with jargon as
1096: such and more to do with academic practice. Practitioners in theory will
1097: often say one thing and do quite another. A theoretical article will
1098: proclaim that it is tackling a problem from a new, exciting perspective
1099: and just churn out the same old approach thinly disguised. Another
1100: article will accuse a rival of a string of theoretical iniquities and
1101: then do exactly the same things itself using different language.
1102: 
1103: Which leads to my final point: theory is difficult, in the last
1104: analysis, because it requires one to think for oneself. When a student
1105: writes a term paper or essay on southwestern Native American pottery, he
1106: or she can churn out a series of ‘facts’ gleaned from the standard
1107: textbooks. Such a list of facts, or more accurately a repetition of the
1108: textbooks’ narratives, may not get a particularly good mark in the
1109: absence of any critical analysis or independent thinking whatsoever, but
1110: the student will get by. Such an approach comes unstuck, however, in
1111: writing a theory essay. It’s more difficult to regurgitate things copied
1112: out of books and not really deeply understood when one is dealing with
1113: abstract ideas, particularly when one writer disagrees so clearly and
1114: fundamentally with another. Though any crop of undergraduate essays will
1115: demonstrate that it is not impossible.
1116: 
1117: The literary theorist Jonathan Culler (1997, 16–17) points out that:
1118: 
1119: theory makes you desire mastery: you hope that theoretical reading will
1120: give you the concepts to organize and understand the phenomena that
1121: concern you. But theory makes mastery impossible, not only because there
1122: is always more to know, but, more specifically and more painfully,
1123: because theory is itself the questioning of presumed results and the
1124: assumptions on which they are based.
1125: 
1126: Theory, then, involves pain. It involves the deliberate placing of
1127: oneself in a vulnerable position, where the need to think for oneself
1128: makes one’s conclusions always provisional and always open to attack
1129: from others.
1130: 
1131: Thinking for oneself, however, is something every student of archaeology
1132: (or any other critical discipline for that matter) is (or should be) in
1133: the business of doing. Ultimately, critical evaluation with reference to
1134: evidence is the key-stone of a liberal education. In an age when
1135: education is increasingly seen as a commodity, in which knowledge can,
1136: it is implied, be bought and sold in the marketplace, the idea of an
1137: education as learning the skills of thinking critically and of
1138: evaluating the evidence for and against a particular view is more and
1139: more under attack. Perhaps it is this cultural context that has led to
1140: some of the sharpness of the ritualized denunciations of theory.
1141: 
1142: Understanding Theory
1143: 
1144: Well, I still feel pretty dubious about theory, but I’m prepared to go
1145: along with you for a bit. Where do we go from here?
1146: 
1147: The rest of this book will try to illuminate some of the major trends in
1148: archaeological theory, starting with the 1960s and moving on from there.
1149: To try to make this book as clear as possible, I am going to adopt two
1150: strategies.
1151: 
1152: First, from time to time I shall talk at length about developments in
1153: associated disciplines and in intellectual thought as a whole. As a
1154: result, long passages and even sub-sections of chapters may seem utterly
1155: irrelevant to the practising archaeologist. The reason I do this is
1156: because archaeology has had a habit of picking up ideas second-hand from
1157: other disciplines. Ideas have been changed, even confused and distorted,
1158: in the process. As a result, it is necessary to go back ‘to source’ to
1159: explain them clearly and to understand precisely how they have been used
1160: and abused by archaeologists. So please bear with the text, plod through
1161: the ‘irrelevant’ material, and I will then try to explain its relevance
1162: to archaeological thought.
1163: 
1164: Second, I shall look at the development of theory historically, looking
1165: first at the origins of the New Archaeology, then at reactions to it. I
1166: suggest that by understanding the historical context of a set of ideas
1167: such as ‘New Archaeology’ or ‘postprocessual archaeology’, one may more
1168: easily sympathize with its aims and grasp some of its underlying
1169: principles and concerns. By understanding this context we can also put
1170: many of the features of contemporary archaeology in their historical
1171: surroundings rather than place them in a vacuum.
1172: 
1173: The next chapter will discuss the New Archaeology; the following three
1174: will look at the questions of ‘science’ and ‘anthropology’ that it
1175: raised. The New Archaeology is now over 40 years old, but the
1176: intellectual questions raised by New Archaeologists are, I will suggest,
1177: absolutely central to contemporary archaeological theory and practice.
1178: 
1179: 2
1180: 
1181: The ‘New Archaeology’
1182: 
1183: Most archaeologists fall in love with the subject by getting ‘hooked’ on
1184: things. The things vary from case to case – castles, Roman baths, flint
1185: or chert arrowheads, Neolithic pots, Maya temples – but in most cases
1186: the immediate appeal is of mystery and romance, of the past calling to
1187: us through its remains. This romantic appeal is often aesthetic and
1188: sensual as well as intellectual. Archaeologists love clambering round
1189: medieval ruins or handling pottery sherds. We try to persuade ourselves,
1190: however, that these ruins or sherds are mere ‘data’. (One colleague told
1191: me that as a result of the acute boredom of his researches he now
1192: loathes Neolithic pottery to the depths of his soul, but I interpret
1193: this as another, rather twisted form of love.) Artefacts, whether as
1194: small as an arrowhead or as large as a royal palace, fascinate us.
1195: 
1196: This love of artefacts, in itself, has nothing to do with archaeology in
1197: the strict sense as the study of the past. Artefacts tell us nothing
1198: about the past in themselves. I have stood in the middle of countless
1199: ruins of castles and ancient palaces and listened very carefully, and
1200: not heard a single syllable. Colleagues tell me that they have had
1201: similar distressing experiences with pottery, bones, bags of seeds. They
1202: love handling and experiencing their material, but it remains silent. In
1203: and of itself, it tells them precisely nothing.
1204: 
1205: Artefacts can’t tell us anything about the past because the past does
1206: not exist. We cannot touch the past, see it or feel it; it is utterly
1207: dead and gone. Our beloved artefacts actually belong to the present.
1208: They exist in the here and now. They may or may not have been made and
1209: used by real people thousands of years ago, but our assessment of the
1210: date of their manufacture and use is itself an assessment that we make,
1211: that is made in the present.
1212: 
1213: Until scientists invent a time machine, the past exists only in the
1214: things we say about it. Archaeologists choose to ask certain questions
1215: of our material: ‘How many beads were found in this grave?’ ‘Do we see a
1216: shift to intensive exploitation of llamas in the Formative Period?’
1217: ‘What was it like to live in the Bronze Age?’ ‘What degree of social
1218: inequality do we see in this period?’ We make general or particular
1219: statements about the past: ‘There was increased use of obsidian in Phase
1220: 3B of this site’; ‘There were more elements of cultural continuity
1221: between Mesolithic and Neolithic populations than have hitherto been
1222: assumed’; ‘Gender relations became less equal through time’; ‘The Romans
1223: were a cruel and vicious people’. These are all statements made here,
1224: now, in the present, as I write and you read. They do not belong to the
1225: past.
1226: 
1227: It is only in works of fiction, or if you believe in ghosts, that past
1228: and present can really be made to collide and merge into each other. It
1229: is striking that many writers have used this collision with great effect
1230: to disturb and horrify the ‘rational’ Western mind (the novels of Peter
1231: Ackroyd are excellent examples of this). More critically, this collision
1232: of past and present is also possible within ‘non-Western’ schemes of
1233: thought, where living cultural traditions may be held to be embodiments
1234: of cultural memory and in which past and present are not so radically
1235: divided; hence in part the conflicts between different cultures over,
1236: for example, excavation and reburial of Native American human remains,
1237: where the belief that time moves in a cycle rather than in a line makes
1238: archaeological excavation a threat to the present through its
1239: ‘desecration’ of the past. ‘Indigenous archaeology’ does not, then,
1240: necessarily make this radical division between present and past, a point
1241: we will return to in chapter 12.
1242: 
1243: Now it is the task of archaeologists to find out about the past. We want
1244: to know what really happened back then. The source materials – stones,
1245: bones, pots – are in the present, and the past that we create is also in
1246: the present. We will never ‘know’ what the past was ‘really like’, but
1247: we all can and do try to write the ‘best’ account we can, an account
1248: that is informed by the evidence that we have and that tries to be
1249: coherent and satisfying to us.
1250: 
1251: One of the basic problems of archaeology, then, is summarized in figure
1252: 2.1. Somehow we have to take the archaeological materials that we have
1253: and through our questioning get them to give us information about the
1254: past. There is a gulf between past and present, a gulf that the
1255: archaeologist has to bridge somehow even if it can never be bridged
1256: securely or definitively. Otherwise we risk a descent into the mindless
1257: activity of simply assembling and collating old objects for their own
1258: sake, rather than as evidence for the past (an activity which has been
1259: derided as ‘mere antiquarianism’, though such a term fails to do justice
1260: to the methods of early antiquarians).
1261: 
1262: I am labouring this point because it is easy to fall into the trap of
1263: believing that the very physicality of archaeological material will in
1264: itself tell us what the past was like. It will not. Kick a megalith and
1265: it hurts; stand in a castle chamber and you see nothing but medieval
1266: fabric. But kicking the megalith or standing in that cold chamber will
1267: tell you nothing about what the Neolithic or the Middle Ages were
1268: ‘really like’, or what processes led to the construction and use of the
1269: megalith or castle. Archaeologists only see megalith and castle in the
1270: present, the here and now. This is a present that is framed by
1271: contemporary ideas, attitudes and assumptions. We see megalith and
1272: castle through our eyes, not the eyes of the prehistoric or medieval
1273: observer.
1274: 
1275: Figure 2.1 The gulf between present and past
1276: 
1277: [c02_image001.jpg]
1278: 
1279: The idea that there is some direct link is closely allied to a belief in
1280: ‘common sense’ discussed in the previous chapter and a naïve empiricism,
1281: in which we do not need theory to translate between present and past.
1282: Such a position, as we have seen, is simply not intellectually
1283: sustainable, but is is nevertheless still widespread in areas of
1284: archaeology that have yet to feel the full impact of explicit theory,
1285: and we will return to it again and again in the chapters that follow.
1286: 
1287: I am also labouring this point because it can be seen as the point of
1288: departure for very different views of archaeological theory. One of the
1289: few areas of common ground for most archaeological theorists is that we
1290: all want to talk about the past, and we all use archaeological material
1291: in the present to do so. But how do we do this? One possible suggestion
1292: is that we should use the methods of the natural sciences on our
1293: material, to try to test alternative hypotheses about past events and
1294: processes against that material and so expand and develop our
1295: understanding. Another possibility is to view artefacts like literary
1296: texts, to ‘read’ them as we would a piece of writing, and so begin to
1297: uncover the rich complexity of past cultural meanings.
1298: 
1299: There are a multiplicity of other views; and the problem has been
1300: perceived for some centuries. Indeed its origins can be traced directly
1301: to the genesis of modern archaeology in the work of Renaissance
1302: antiquarians. The English humanist Sir Thomas Browne centred his
1303: wonderful mid-seventeenth century essay Hydriotaphia around the
1304: discovery of cremation urns that we now believe to be of Anglo-Saxon
1305: origin, fifth to seventh century ad in date. Browne contrasted the
1306: physical solidity of the urns with the impossibility of understanding
1307: the religious beliefs that they expressed, or even the impossibility of
1308: assigning a date to them (he speculated that they might be Roman: figure
1309: 2.2).
1310: 
1311: The question of how to link present and past, however, surfaced in its
1312: most explicit form with the New Archaeology of the 1960s and 1970s.
1313: Though now over 40 years old, the New Archaeology raised foundational
1314: questions about the nature of archaeology that form the basis of many
1315: theoretical views today.
1316: 
1317: Culture History
1318: 
1319: There is an ongoing debate about the nature of archaeological theory
1320: before the New Archaeology. There is not space here to do this debate
1321: justice.
1322: 
1323: Some historians of archaeology maintain that the century before 1960 was
1324: the ‘long sleep’ of archaeological theory, in which very little explicit
1325: discussion of theory took place. They argue that archaeologists
1326: concentrated on collecting masses of archaeological material within an
1327: unquestioned, generally assumed framework. Others deny this, maintaining
1328: that this period did see lively theoretical debates of various kinds.
1329: They further maintain that the importance of the New Archaeology for the
1330: development of archaeological thought has been grossly overestimated. In
1331: particular, they point to New Archaeology’s origins and impact in
1332: Britain and North America, and its much more limited impact in many
1333: other parts of the world.
1334: 
1335: I want to set this important historical debate aside, and stress here
1336: that one of the starting points of the so-called ‘New Archaeology’ lay
1337: in what I have discussed above, that is in the notion that mere data
1338: collection – the acquisition of more stuff – does not in itself lead to
1339: a better understanding of the past. David Clarke, one of the principal
1340: proponents of the New Archaeology, started his classic book Analytical
1341: Archaeology with a telling quote from Lewis Carroll:
1342: 
1343: Now here, you see, it takes all the running you can do, to keep in the
1344: same place. (The Queen to Alice, Through the Looking-Glass, Chapter 2,
1345: Lewis Carroll, 1832–98.)
1346: 
1347: Every year produces a fresh crop of archaeological excavations, a new
1348: harvest of prehistoric artefacts. … The archaeologists come and go, new
1349: names and sites outshine the old, while hundreds of years of collected
1350: material overflows and submerges our museum storerooms. At the same time
1351: a relentless current of articles and books describe and label the new
1352: material so that the intrepid archaeologist, by dint of furious
1353: activity, can just maintain his [sic] status quo against the constant
1354: stream of data. However, the nebulous doubt arises in our minds that a
1355: modern empirical discipline ought to be able to aim at more rewarding
1356: results than the maintenance of a relative status quo and a steady flow
1357: of counterfeit history books. (Clarke 1978: 3)
1358: 
1359: Clarke, then, was not at all sure that the methods of archaeologists
1360: actually give us better and more reliable versions of the past. In his
1361: view, archaeologists seem to dig up more and more things but stay in the
1362: same place in terms of our ideas. Our knowledge of artefacts in the
1363: present grows better and better, but because we do not bridge the gulf
1364: with the past very well, progress in understanding that past does not
1365: follow.
1366: 
1367: Figure 2.2 Illustration of burial urns from Sir Thomas Browne,
1368: Hydriotaphia (1658). The Latin tag is from the Latin poet Propertius:
1369: ‘See, I am a burden which is lifted by five fingers’
1370: 
1371: [c02_image002.jpg]
1372: 
1373: What were the theories Clarke was dissatisfied with? In other words, how
1374: did archaeologists before the 1960s, and how do many archaeologists
1375: continuing to work in culture-historical traditions today, translate
1376: archaeological material into statements about the past? It is easy to
1377: over-generalize here, and exceptions can always be found. One of the
1378: basic building-blocks, however, was the idea of an archaeological
1379: culture and what it meant in terms of past human populations. In the
1380: words of Gordon Childe:
1381: 
1382: We find certain types of remains – pots, implements, ornaments, burial
1383: rites, and house forms – constantly recurring together. Such a complex
1384: of associated traits we shall term a ‘cultural group’ or just a
1385: ‘culture’. We assume that such a complex is the material expression of
1386: what today would be called a ‘people’. (Childe 1929: v–vi)
1387: 
1388: Such an idea of culture has been called normative. That is, it depends
1389: on two assumptions: first, that artefacts are expressions of cultural
1390: norms, ideas in people’s heads, and second, that those norms define what
1391: ‘culture’ is. I will give two examples, one from the present and one
1392: from the past.
1393: 
1394: 1 The English are the English because they drink tea, speak English,
1395: don’t eat horse, and queue in an orderly fashion, often for hours
1396: without complaint. This distinguishes them from the French who drink
1397: coffee, speak French, eat horse and do not queue with such equanimity.
1398: (These are, of course, all cultural norms, ideas about what is the right
1399: way to behave, and one can easily see that they are in fact ideals (in
1400: this case crude stereotypes) that don’t necessarily correspond to
1401: reality in every case.)
1402: 
1403: 2 The Linearbandkeramik (LBK) archaeological culture differs from the
1404: Trichterbandkeramik (TRB) in Neolithic Europe: in LBK areas we find
1405: rectangular house forms, pottery decorated with linear designs, a
1406: certain form of arable economy. In TRB areas house form, pottery
1407: decoration and economy are all different. (Again, this is an ideal: not
1408: every TRB or LBK site will share all the features of all the others.)
1409: 
1410: Such a concept of culture is also polythetic: that is, it depends on a
1411: number of different traits occurring together rather than on one trait
1412: alone. Drinking coffee does not make an English person French; a single
1413: rectangular house does not turn a TRB settlement into an LBK village. It
1414: is a number of traits occurring together, as Childe stressed, that
1415: defines culture. In North America, lists of traits were tabulated and
1416: added up from site to site.
1417: 
1418: So to summarize, in the traditional view we translate present into past
1419: by collecting artefacts into groups, and naming those groups as
1420: archaeological cultures. We then make the equation between an
1421: archaeological culture and a human culture by making the assumption that
1422: artefacts are expressions of cultural ideas or norms. (It is a tenable
1423: position, sometimes taken, to only go half-way with this process: in
1424: other words, to classify and name archaeological cultures, but to deny
1425: an equation with human cultures.)
1426: 
1427: This approach, which was termed ‘culture history’ by many, has several
1428: consequences. In the first place, it leads to a tendency to
1429: particularize what archaeologists say about the past rather than
1430: generalize. What this means is that instead of stressing the
1431: similarities between things, one stressed the differences and
1432: particularities between them.
1433: 
1434: One might, for example, want to generalize between the LBK and TRB, to
1435: stress that these different groups were at the same ‘level’ of social or
1436: economic development. They might both be classified as societies with a
1437: certain level of social ranking, for example, or with certain similar
1438: modes of subsistence economy, or exchange and trade. The
1439: culture-oriented approach, however, tends to direct attention away from
1440: such general features towards what makes the TRB and LBK distinctive,
1441: both from each other and from other cultures. It encourages us to stress
1442: their differences, their diagnostic and peculiar features, their
1443: particular forms of house type and burial rite, the contrast between the
1444: zigging of one form of pottery decoration and the zagging of another
1445: form, rather than those they have in common.
1446: 
1447: The second consequence of a normative view of culture is that cultures
1448: tend to be viewed as unchanging. To repeat, the normative approach sees
1449: artefacts as expressions of shared ideas. If people in the LBK all
1450: shared the same ideas on how to build houses, make pots and bury their
1451: dead, where did change come from? The easiest way to explain change is
1452: to suggest that it was brought in from outside, from another human
1453: group. Such outside ‘influence’ can be of one of two forms: migration of
1454: peoples, or of diffusion the spread of ideas through contact between
1455: groups.
1456: 
1457: Figure 2.3 ‘Cultures’ in space and time, from Childe (1929)
1458: 
1459: [c02_image003.jpg]
1460: 
1461: Accounts of prehistory written using a culture-historical framework,
1462: then, tend to consist of two elements. The first is a chronological
1463: sequence of cultures, a sort of timetable with culture groups listed
1464: instead of trains. The second is a map full of arrows to indicate the
1465: migration and diffusion of ideas that marked change between cultures
1466: (see for example figures 2.3 and 2.4).
1467: 
1468: The whole synthesis thus produced tends to be descriptive. That is, it
1469: describes phases and areas of cultural change: this culture followed
1470: that culture, this innovation spread or diffused at that rate. Much
1471: traditional prehistory reads like much traditional history, that is like
1472: a chronicle of events held together by a narrative. Often, there is
1473: little explicit explanation of why this or that pottery style changed,
1474: why this or that culture spread or changed.
1475: 
1476: Figure 2.4 Piggott’s (1968) view of culture. Piggott’s caption reads:
1477: ‘Distribution of mature La Téne culture and influences; 2, Sword
1478: scabbards in Swiss style; 3, British derivatives; 4, Many scabbards’
1479: 
1480: [c02_image004.jpg]
1481: 
1482: This descriptive emphasis is one reason why culture-historical
1483: approaches continue to be popular in Classical, medieval and historical
1484: archaeology: in these areas, underlying causes or reasons for change are
1485: often implicitly invoked not from the archaeological material itself,
1486: but from documents and wider historical narratives. Thus, a series of
1487: pieces of art or examples of tombs or architectural features will be
1488: described, with change then being ascribed to the ‘influence of Rome’ or
1489: the ‘spread of Renaissance values’ (see chapter 5).
1490: 
1491: In some regional and national traditions, for example in much central
1492: and eastern European work, a well-established and continuing approach is
1493: to use typologies and distribution maps to sort artefacts into
1494: archaeological groups and cultures in time and space, without then
1495: making the step of moving outwards to statements about human cultures.
1496: Rather, the archaeological entities thus defined can then be gathered
1497: together and synthesized with evidence from historical linguistics and
1498: historical ethnography to form an overarching discipline of
1499: ‘prehistory’, within which archaeology is one of several contributing
1500: techniques, alongside historical ethnography, historical linguistics and
1501: other disciplines such as folklife studies. In North America, the
1502: ‘direct historical method’ worked backwards from ‘known’ Native American
1503: groups from ethnography and history, mapping them in terms of
1504: archaeological cultures, and working backwards to a prehistoric origin
1505: point.
1506: 
1507: Lewis Binford called the culture-historical view of change ‘an aquatic
1508: view of culture’ (Binford 1964, 204). Binford’s image was a caricature,
1509: but like all good caricatures it contained an essence of truth. What
1510: Binford meant was that the traditional archaeologists of his time saw
1511: the map of the prehistoric world as being a little like a large pool of
1512: water. When an innovation was made for whatever reason in a given place,
1513: it would tend to spread through the process of ‘influence’ or diffusion
1514: in all directions, like the ripples from a stone dropped in the pool. In
1515: any given location, then, one sees cross-cutting ‘ripples’ of influence.
1516: 
1517: I want to repeat that this characterization of culture-historical
1518: archaeology is far too brief, over-simplified and over-generalized.
1519: Suggestions for further accounts are given in the Further Reading
1520: section. In particular, it leans too heavily on what New Archaeologists
1521: such as David Clarke, Colin Renfrew and Lewis Binford said that
1522: traditional archaeology was like. Many have complained that the New
1523: Archaeology set up a distorted image of traditional thought for its own
1524: polemical purposes, a ‘straw person’ that could easily be knocked down.
1525: But if this is what New Archaeology thought traditional thinking was
1526: like, this was what New Archaeology was reacting against.
1527: 
1528: Origins of the New Archaeology
1529: 
1530: What, then, was the ‘New Archaeology’? One thing is certain: it is no
1531: longer new. The term ‘New Archaeology’ is applied to a school of thought
1532: that swept through much of Anglo-American archaeology in the 1960s and
1533: early 1970s. It must be seen in the contexts of similar currents of
1534: thought in other disciplines – the New Geography in particular.
1535: 
1536: The ‘New Archaeology’ was also not a single set of beliefs or theories.
1537: One does not expect all members of a political party to have identical
1538: views on all policy matters; there are rather certain core ideas and
1539: values that inform the approach of any one party. Similarly, under the
1540: banner of the New Archaeology was a very diverse set of archaeologists
1541: with different approaches and beliefs. What united them all was a sense
1542: of dissatisfaction with the way archaeology was going, a sense that
1543: things had to change and that they, as a thrusting new generation of
1544: ‘young Turks’, were going to change it.
1545: 
1546: This dissatisfaction with traditional archaeology was crystallized in
1547: the phrase: ‘we must be more scientific and more anthropological’. In
1548: this one phrase can be seen New Archaeology’s source of disaffection
1549: with what went before, the foundations of its rise, and in my view the
1550: seeds of subsequent developments.
1551: 
1552: Why was culture history seen as unscientific? We must go back to David
1553: Clarke’s comments on running harder and harder to stay in the same
1554: place. More data do not automatically equate to better ideas about the
1555: past. Archaeologists spend much of their time accumulating more and more
1556: information and presenting that information in ever more detail – what
1557: is sometimes called in German Materialschlacht, or literally ‘war of the
1558: materials’. This does not automatically mean however a better and better
1559: idea of what the past was like, for all the reasons discussed above.
1560: Such studies simply fit more and more archaeological material into the
1561: same existing classification, for example an endless sequence of
1562: cultures. The end result is not necessarily a better or more accurate
1563: picture of the past in any way.
1564: 
1565: ‘Science’, argued the New Archaeologists, uses its data to test
1566: hypotheses about the way the world works, and generalizes from these
1567: conclusions. ‘Science’ progresses; it does not simply collate its facts
1568: into orderly patterns, rather it confronts theory and data in such a way
1569: as to make larger and larger and deeper and deeper its understanding of
1570: the world. The natural sciences, in this view, have a proven record of
1571: developing better and better understandings of the world around us.
1572: 
1573: Why was culture history seen as not anthropological? Anthropology is the
1574: study of humanity. Traditional archaeologists, in the basic process of
1575: sorting artefacts into groups and cultures, often seemed to ignore human
1576: beings: pottery styles seemed to get up and march around with no
1577: reference to the humans that produced them. In this sense many forms of
1578: culture history can be seen as fetishistic. A fetish is a thing that
1579: comes to stand for something else such as a human or a human group:
1580: traditional archaeologists often seem to spend their time describing the
1581: movement of these things without thinking about the human beings, the
1582: cultural systems, behind them. In the shorthand of culture history,
1583: pottery styles and house types seem to develop little legs and run
1584: around without any help from human beings.
1585: 
1586: At a deeper level, traditional archaeology was not anthropological in
1587: the sense that there seemed to be no guarantee that the archaeological
1588: ‘cultures’ so lovingly produced by culture historians had any
1589: relationship to real human communities. Many New Archaeologists
1590: questioned the link between archaeological cultures – Childe’s recurring
1591: assemblages of traits – and past peoples. Childe himself had come to
1592: doubt whether we really could equate archaeological cultures and past
1593: peoples:
1594: 
1595: It would be rash to define precisely what sort of social group
1596: corresponds to the archaeologist’s ‘culture’. … Culture and language
1597: need not coincide. (Childe 1942: 26–7)
1598: 
1599: This is why many New Archaeologists moved away from the normative
1600: conception of culture and looked for other ways to explain the things we
1601: dug up. Clarke’s famous 1973 article claimed that archaeology
1602: experienced a ‘loss of innocence’. The innocent equations archaeology
1603: had made between artefacts, cultures and peoples, or between data and
1604: interpretation more generally, had to be questioned.
1605: 
1606: To repeat, the New Archaeology must be understood as a movement or mood
1607: of dissatisfaction rather than as a specific set of beliefs. David
1608: Clarke called it ‘a set of questions rather than a set of answers’
1609: (1973). It was certainly marked with revolutionary fervour. Lewis
1610: Binford, its most famous figure, tells a story that sums up both this
1611: fervour and its irritation with the particularism of traditional
1612: methods:
1613: 
1614: I remember one day when one of the traditional Griffin students had
1615: returned from a field trip to the Upper Illinois valley. He had burst
1616: into the museum with the announcement that he had found a ‘unique’ item,
1617: a negative-painted sherd from the site. Griffin was obviously
1618: stimulated, and Papworth said, ‘Let me see’. He took the sherd, looked
1619: at it and then threw it on the floor and ground it to pieces with the
1620: heel of his shoe. ‘That’s what I think of your “unique” sherd’. Griffin
1621: was in total shock, the student was practically in tears, and I was
1622: laughing inside. (Binford 1972: 130–1)
1623: 
1624: New Archaeology: Key Points
1625: 
1626: As the New Archaeology developed, certain key themes came to be repeated
1627: in the writings of its proponents. I will try to summarize these under
1628: seven points. If the descriptions seem brief and over-simplified, many
1629: of these themes will be discussed and developed further in future
1630: chapters. The important thing here is to get an overall sense of the
1631: spirit of the movement.
1632: 
1633: First, an emphasis on cultural evolution. The word ‘evolution’ has a
1634: series of different though related meanings stretching back to the work
1635: of Darwin in the nineteenth century (see chapters 9 and 10). For some
1636: within New Archaeology, it meant in part that societies could be
1637: classified on a scale from simple to complex. Cultures in this view
1638: evolved from one state to another, for example from ‘band’ societies to
1639: ‘tribal’ networks to ‘chiefdoms’. This stress was in part a rejection of
1640: the aquatic view of culture, with its random ripples spreading across
1641: the map. Instead New Archaeologists wanted to look at the internal
1642: dynamics of a society, what was driving its general direction of social
1643: development (the phrase often used was ‘cultural trajectory’).
1644: 
1645: Evolution was also part of a conscious stress on generalities rather
1646: than particularities. Cultures might differ in their specific forms of
1647: jewellery and house type, one pottery style might zig and the other zag.
1648: But both societies might be comparable on the same level on an
1649: evolutionary scale. So we could generalize about, say, the evolution of
1650: state level societies from chiefdom level societies, without worrying
1651: too much about the different art styles or pottery decoration in each
1652: case.
1653: 
1654: Figure 2.5 David Clarke’s (1976) systemic view of culture. In language
1655: characteristic of the New Archaeology, Clark’s caption reads: ‘A static
1656: and schematic model of the dynamic equilibrium between the subsystem
1657: networks of a single sociocultural system and its total environmental
1658: system. S_(n) represents the summation of the effects of alien
1659: sociocultural systems connected to S by cultural ‘coactions’ (dashed
1660: lines) and to the environment by ‘interactions’ (solid lines). To set
1661: the model in motion all the components must oscillate randomly along
1662: intercorrelated trending trajectories’
1663: 
1664: [c02_image005.jpg]
1665: 
1666: Second, an emphasis on systems thinking (see figure 2.5 and chapter 5).
1667: Culture wasn’t just a mixed bag of different randomly acquired norms, as
1668: culture history had implied; rather it was a system. Lewis Binford
