1669: (1964) defined culture as ‘man’s extrasomatic means of adaptation’.
1670: 
1671: To clarify Binford’s point, other animals are adapted to their
1672: environment through their bodies – the giraffe has a long neck to reach
1673: to the tops of trees in the savannah, polar bears have lots of fur to
1674: deal with the Arctic winter. Humans adapt through culture – the Inuit
1675: wear furs and live in igloos, the !Kung San have an extensive range of
1676: specialized hunting equipment. These cultural adaptations are all
1677: outside (extra) the body (soma), hence ‘extrasomatic’.
1678: 
1679: So past cultures were:
1680: 
1681: (a) not just a bag of different, randomly acquired norms – the different
1682: parts of the system were related one to another as part of a functioning
1683: system;
1684: 
1685: (b) comparable to other kinds of system such as those found in the
1686: physical and animal worlds.
1687: 
1688: I will talk more about systemic approaches in chapter 5. But note here
1689: that it allowed New Archaeology to do two things. It firstly helped the
1690: stress on generalization. Different cultures may have had different
1691: pottery styles and burial rites, but the underlying social system could
1692: be shown to have underlying similarities (they were both more or less
1693: complex, practised similar systems of gift exchange even if the specific
1694: goods traded were different, and so on).
1695: 
1696: It secondly helped New Archaeologists to be more optimistic about what
1697: archaeology could achieve. One of the least attractive features of
1698: traditional archaeology was its implied pessimism: we can never
1699: reconstruct the religious or social life of past peoples, we can only
1700: build chronologies. Christopher Hawkes famously wrote of seven levels of
1701: inference in archaeology, ranging from technology and subsistence to the
1702: almost unknowable areas of society and religion. James Deetz questioned
1703: this:
1704: 
1705: Stress on the essential interrelatedness of cultural systems allows us
1706: to reach understandings of many aspects from a relative few … this
1707: certainty is at least a partial answer to the problems posed by the
1708: incompleteness of the archaeological record. (Deetz 1972: 112)
1709: 
1710: Third, if culture was adaptive, it was adaptive to an external
1711: environment. For the archaeologist Kent Flannery, we were not just
1712: looking at objects and trying to learn about human cultures through
1713: them; we were trying to reconstruct the whole ecological system behind
1714: both ‘the Indian and the artefact’ (1973b). This theoretical stress on
1715: the importance of the external environment led to interest in cultural
1716: materialism (in which the material world is seen as more important than
1717: the mental world), cultural ecology, and modelling of the subsistence
1718: economy. Here, new theoretical attitudes went hand-in-hand with the new
1719: scientific techniques that were developed in the postwar period: faunal
1720: analysis, palaeoethnobotany, carbon-14 dating, dendrochronology, and so
1721: on.
1722: 
1723: Fourth, there was stress on a scientific approach. New Archaeologists
1724: saw traditional archaeology as modelling itself on the techniques of
1725: traditional history, with cultures taking the place of historical actors
1726: and the aim being the reconstruction of individual events in time. This
1727: may not have been a fair characterization, either of what archaeologists
1728: actually did or what traditional historians actually do, but it led New
1729: Archaeologists in part to embrace Science as the way forward. According
1730: to Watson, Redman and LeBlanc in their Explanation in Archaeology: An
1731: Explicitly Scientific Approach (1971), one index of the progress of
1732: archaeology is the extent to which hypotheses are scientifically tested.
1733: 
1734: Fifth, there was stress on the idea of culture process. The idea of
1735: ‘process’ was central to New Archaeology and remains absolutely central
1736: to much of archaeological theory, but it is a difficult idea to grasp.
1737: It involves several related themes that have already been touched on: we
1738: want to explain rather than be merely descriptive – to ask ‘why?’ rather
1739: than merely ‘when?’; we want to look at the underlying process rather
1740: than the ‘noise’ on top; we want to look at change in the long term. I
1741: will examine the concept of process in more detail in chapter 5.
1742: 
1743: Many New Archaeologists contrasted culture process to culture history.
1744: In this view, traditional history simply described a set of more or less
1745: random political events such as battles and births and deaths of
1746: monarchs without ever really explaining anything. By substituting
1747: process for history, the long-term trends or processes beneath the
1748: surface of such events became the important objects of study.
1749: 
1750: Sixth and more generally, there was a trend to attempt to become more
1751: explicit about one’s biases. A good scientist, argued New
1752: Archaeologists, does not use unspoken and implicit assumptions; he or
1753: she makes clear their aims and interests. Much of the New Archaeology
1754: was concerned with making what had been unspoken formally outlined. A
1755: good example is the technique of typology, or the classification of
1756: objects according to their changing form. It has long been accepted that
1757: one could classify objects, and that this classification has something
1758: to do with chronological order: archaeologists make statements like
1759: ‘pottery vessels get baggier through time’, or ‘this jewellery style
1760: goes out of fashion in this period’ as a matter of course. But the
1761: criteria by which a typologist suggests one piece of jewellery or
1762: architectural style is ‘early’ and another one is ‘late’ are rarely
1763: clearly spelt out (in fact, they often tend to be implicitly derived
1764: from ideas and concepts developed by nineteenth-century art historians).
1765: 
1766: In his book Analytical Archaeology, David Clarke used many of the
1767: concepts of traditional archaeology – typology, assemblage and culture
1768: for example – but discussed openly, explicitly and at length how they
1769: should be defined. For Clarke, the need to be explicit and precise in
1770: one’s terms to replace ‘the murky exhalation that passes for
1771: “interpretative thinking” in archaeology’ was one of the main thrusts of
1772: the New Archaeology.
1773: 
1774: A related aspect of making one’s biases explicit was problem
1775: orientation, or the belief that one should survey and dig sites, or do
1776: research more generally, with clear research questions in mind. Again,
1777: this linked in with being scientific – the scientist tested specific
1778: hypotheses, he or she brought specific questions to the data. In chapter
1779: 3, we will see how asking specific questions or testing specific
1780: hypotheses was central to the idea of archaeology as a science.
1781: 
1782: A seventh and final concern of New Archaeology was the understanding of
1783: variability. What this meant was a basic understanding of our material
1784: in statistical terms. Previous archaeologists had often concentrated on
1785: the biggest and best sites, or the most beautiful artefacts. New
1786: Archaeologists pointed out that we couldn’t understand, say, a major
1787: urban civilization without looking at its rural infrastructure (the
1788: importance of looking at the whole system again), and we couldn’t
1789: understand the rural infrastructure without knowing just how many rural
1790: sites there were on the ground. So to really get to grips with
1791: understanding that civilization, the archaeologist might want to forgo
1792: digging yet another elite site with lots of pretty, exotic artefacts and
1793: instead concentrate on systematic survey of ordinary farmsteads. The
1794: finds might be less spectacular, but the understanding of the settlement
1795: system would be much greater.
1796: 
1797: To understand variability, New Archaeologists looked much more
1798: critically at sampling theory and techniques. To be sure of variability,
1799: at the very least we had to be sure we were looking at a representative
1800: sample of sites. Kent Flannery told a memorable parable of a
1801: Mesoamerican archaeologist who only stopped to record archaeological
1802: sites if he was forced to put his Jeep into first gear to get over the
1803: top of them. In response, New Archaeologists explored methods of
1804: sampling randomly or at least systematically.
1805: 
1806: Many of the worst aspects of the traditional attitudes the New
1807: Archaeology castigated still survive and indeed flourish today. A few
1808: years ago I remember asking a prominent, senior scholar who had written
1809: several influential books on traditional architecture how many houses of
1810: a certain date and type there were in a certain county of England. ‘Oh’,
1811: he said, ‘an awful lot’. Yes, but how many? Tens, hundreds, thousands?
1812: ‘Well, a lot, but I really couldn’t put a figure to it’ came the reply.
1813: And how many were there in an adjacent area? ‘Oh, not many at all …’.
1814: And what, statistically, was the proportion of Type A houses to Type B
1815: houses in the two areas? ‘Ah …’. And yet you are quite confident in
1816: saying that Type A houses are more frequently found than Type B houses
1817: in this area but not that, and we can indeed go on from this observation
1818: to draw conclusions about relative levels of wealth in the two areas?
1819: ‘Oh yes, that’s quite clear …’. One does not have to be a paid-up New
1820: Archaeologist to see that such reasoning is an insecure foundation for
1821: any understanding of the archaeological record.
1822: 
1823: I’m a bit sceptical about all this. In the first place, archaeologists
1824: before New Archaeology were not the dry, traditional dullards that New
1825: Archaeologists made out. They did many of these things already. Look,
1826: for example, at Grahame Clark’s work at Star Carr, where environmental
1827: analysis was used in the 1950s. Or Alfred Kidder’s work in Mesoamerica
1828: and the American Southwest. And Gordon Childe can hardly be accused of
1829: many of the sins the New Archaeology tried to pin on culture historians.
1830: His books Man Makes Himself and What Happened in History are full of
1831: dynamic pictures – they try to explain things, they deal with underlying
1832: processes …
1833: 
1834: Your analysis contains a lot of truth. Traditional archaeology is not
1835: necessarily the narrowly descriptive, sterile pursuit that New
1836: Archaeology painted it. It can be argued, for example, that traditional
1837: accounts of migration and diffusion do address ‘why?’ questions rather
1838: than simply describe the data. There are ongoing debates within such
1839: traditions on the concept of ethnogenesis and the nature of historical
1840: change. Additionally, books like Childe’s The Prehistory of European
1841: Society presented a model in which Indigenous cultures were seen as
1842: creative and dynamic and in which modified diffusion played a key role
1843: in explanation. Again, in North America the ‘culture history’ model was
1844: in part caricatured by New Archaeology.
1845: 
1846: Nevertheless New Archaeology’s criticisms were pertinent. For our
1847: purposes in trying to understand where theory is today, it isn’t of
1848: primary importance to ascertain whether New Archaeology was accurate in
1849: its criticisms; we want to understand why New Archaeology developed in
1850: the way it did. Our all-too-brief sketch serves for this purpose.
1851: 
1852: It is equally true that much of New Archaeology was not really new. Like
1853: so many theoretical movements in archaeology, it borrowed from other
1854: disciplines. The work of the cultural anthropologist Leslie White was
1855: especially influential. His 1949 book The Science of Culture stressed
1856: the need for a scientific approach and the idea of culture as a system.
1857: Again, another anthropologist, Julian Steward, had stressed cultural
1858: ecology and adaptation in his work. Binford’s early work makes clear his
1859: profound debt to both White and Steward. Finally, stress on systems
1860: thinking owed much to Walter Taylor’s emphasis on what he called a
1861: ‘conjunctive approach’ in his 1948 A Study of Archeology. But don’t
1862: forget that White, Steward and Taylor were in a minority within their
1863: own disciplines. White in particular was writing against the orthodox
1864: cultural anthropology of his time when his thinking was adopted by
1865: archaeologists.
1866: 
1867: The New Archaeology, particularly in its stress on anthropology, can be
1868: particularly associated with the New World. In Britain New
1869: Archaeologists such as Clarke and Renfrew had a great impact;
1870: Kristiansen, Randsborg and others applied New Archaeology to
1871: Scandinavian archaeology, and there was some take-up in other areas of
1872: Europe, for example Spain. Overall, how ever, the impact of the New
1873: Archaeology was not as revolutionary or as hard-hitting as in North
1874: America. I suggest there were several reasons for this.
1875: 
1876: One is the institutional set-up of archaeology. In North America, there
1877: are few Departments of Archaeology. The majority of academic
1878: archaeologists have posts within Departments of Anthropology, and form a
1879: ‘minority’ within those departments. Some archaeologists are hosted in
1880: Classics departments, but (with exceptions) these archaeologists are not
1881: always as engaged in theoretical discussion as their ‘anthropological’
1882: counterparts. By contrast, in Britain Departments of Archaeology are
1883: largely separate institutions, or are more closely linked to Departments
1884: of History. In continental Europe, archaeologists again often find
1885: themselves within Institutes of Pre- and Proto-History rather than
1886: having primary links with anthropology, though links with ethnography
1887: and folklife of European peoples are often found.
1888: 
1889: Hence, North American graduate students intending to do their PhDs on an
1890: archaeological topic and go on to an academic career will generally be
1891: expected to read extensively and take courses in anthropological theory,
1892: where they will encounter many of the ideas of evolution and
1893: cross-cultural comparison discussed above. By contrast, a European
1894: graduate student will probably have less theoretical training in general
1895: and often very little encounter with anthropological method.
1896: 
1897: Underlying this institutional difference are different perceptions of
1898: the past. American archaeology is split between study of ‘native’ New
1899: World cultures and the Old World and the ‘historical archaeology’ of its
1900: colonies, to the extent that Native American archaeology is often spelt
1901: differently – ‘archeology’. The anthropologist Franz Boas played a
1902: pivotal role in keeping archaeology within the ambit of anthropology in
1903: North America in the earlier twentieth century. Now anthropology has
1904: traditionally been concerned with ‘other cultures’ rather than with
1905: ‘us’. As relatively few Native Americans were employed within American
1906: archaeology until very recently, New World cultures have been perceived
1907: very much as ‘them’. In the past, archaeological remains such as burial
1908: mounds and cliff dwellings had been interpreted as evidence for the Lost
1909: Tribes of Israel or analogous groups, it being assumed that Native
1910: Americans were too ‘primitive’ to have produced such cultures; but such
1911: theories had been completely abandoned by the archaeological profession
1912: by the 1960s. This situation is changing, for example with increased
1913: recruitment and training of Native Americans as archaeologists and with
1914: a changing political and cultural context (see chapter 12), but this
1915: institutional and cultural history remains powerful in determining
1916: archaeology’s position in North American cultural and intellectual life.
1917: 
1918: For British and other European archaeologists the situation is
1919: different. The prehistoric site of Stonehenge is used as a symbol of
1920: ‘English Heritage’, though it was built 2–3,000 years before the
1921: ‘English’, according to traditional history, arrived on what is now the
1922: English coast. The British, like Europeans generally, perceive
1923: prehistoric archaeology as part of ‘their’ past, part of their
1924: ‘heritage’. Right or wrong, this perception has meant that both past and
1925: present archaeological interpretation has been thoroughly permeated with
1926: nationalist concerns, often of a politically unpleasant variety. Also,
1927: prehistory and history are seen as much more of a continuum: Neolithic
1928: Greece leads to the Minoans and Myceneans, and Iron Age studies blend
1929: into the Roman and early medieval periods. There is therefore not such a
1930: radical divide between prehistoric and historical archaeology.
1931: 
1932: So British archaeologists tend not to worry so much about the need to
1933: make explicitly theoretical generalizations in order to justify their
1934: work, and are much more comfortable with the idea of archaeology as the
1935: ‘handmaiden of history’. New Archaeology had even less impact in
1936: continental Europe than it did in Britain, again for complex historical
1937: reasons. It also had less influence on the study of later periods: ‘New
1938: Archaeology’ had a major impact on the study of European prehistory,
1939: arguably most of all on the Palaeolithic, but less on Roman, medieval
1940: and post-medieval archaeology, to the extent that I would characterize
1941: the majority of European medieval and later archaeology as remaining
1942: culture-historical in method, tone and emphasis.
1943: 
1944: I think that there was also a practical element in the differential
1945: impact of New Archaeology in different areas of the globe. Stand in the
1946: middle of the Arizona desert and the need for sampling theory,
1947: understanding of variability, and so on is all too clear. By contrast,
1948: British and other European landscapes are cramped; they have been
1949: intensively settled for millennia, and intensively studied for
1950: centuries. Most of their basic units (administrative boundaries, patches
1951: of woodland) are irregular in shape and are themselves hundreds if not
1952: thousands of years old. As a result, many of the techniques of New
1953: Archaeology such as sampling theory make clear sense as practical
1954: strategy in the Arizona desert, but are counter-intuitive in the densely
1955: settled palimpsest that is Wessex.
1956: 
1957: Anyway, what happened to all this youthful enthusiasm? I bet it didn’t
1958: get very far.
1959: 
1960: Many of the ways in which New Archaeology developed will be picked up in
1961: later chapters. One thing that inevitably happened is that New
1962: Archaeologists got older. From being pushy young research students
1963: intent on causing a fuss, they became tenured lecturers and professors
1964: with job security, positions on powerful committees and funding bodies
1965: and supervising pushy graduate students of their own. Indeed, many of
1966: the key youthful figures in New Archaeology fostered groups of students
1967: who are today’s generation of senior academics.
1968: 
1969: As New Archaeology got older and developed as a body of thought it
1970: became known as processualism. Processualism is so called because of its
1971: stress on culture process (point five above); the term will be discussed
1972: further in chapter 5.
1973: 
1974: Case Study: The Enigma of the Megaliths
1975: 
1976: An excellent example of the way New Archaeology led to new ways of
1977: thinking about the past was that of megalithic tombs in Western Europe.
1978: These are burial monuments built with large stones, within which
1979: collective burials often took place.
1980: 
1981: Traditional studies of megaliths had concentrated on dating, typology
1982: and diffusion. Scholars such as Glyn Daniel and P. Bosch-Gimpera had
1983: defined megaliths into sub-groups based on their position around the
1984: Western Mediterranean and Atlantic seaboard (figure 2.6). They tried to
1985: date these groups in the absence of scientific dating techniques such as
1986: carbon-14; the only way of doing this was to look for similarities in
1987: the form of the monuments and assume that similarities had been produced
1988: through contact. Ultimately, then, megaliths could be dated by reference
1989: to their presumed ancestors in the Mediterranean such as the Maltese
1990: temples. These could then be cross-dated in turn with reference to the
1991: literate civilizations of the Eastern Mediterranean. There was a clear
1992: link here between theory (the importance of typology and the dominant
1993: idea of diffusion) and archaeological practice (the need to set up a
1994: working chronology, without which we couldn’t really say anything at
1995: all).
1996: 
1997: Colin Renfrew questioned this work. He used the tree-ring calibration of
1998: radiocarbon dates to show that the megaliths on the Atlantic seaboard
1999: were in fact much older than their supposed ‘origins’ in the
2000: Mediterranean. Some scholars responded by simply reversing the arrows so
2001: that the ‘influence’ went in the opposite direction, but Renfrew
2002: suggested that instead of trying to prove or disprove diffusionary
2003: links, we should instead ask why the monuments were built in the first
2004: place. In short, we should look less at chronology and diffusion and
2005: more at the underlying process involved. Or to put it another way, we
2006: should not be content with dating and describing; we should try to
2007: explain the phenomenon of the megaliths.
2008: 
2009: Figure 2.6 Glyn Daniel’s (1941) view of megalith origins
2010: 
2011: [c02_image006.jpg]
2012: 
2013: Renfrew suggested that megaliths might be territorial markers. He
2014: pointed out that whereas early agricultural communities moving from east
2015: to west across Europe had plenty of land, being able to simply expand
2016: westwards into new territory when things got cramped, when such peoples
2017: reached the Atlantic coast there was nowhere left to go. Additionally,
2018: these areas also had a high population level in the preceding Mesolithic
2019: period, populations who would also be pressing for territory. In such a
2020: situation he suggested that different communities would be competing for
2021: land. The megalithic tombs, then, would mark out the land of particular
2022: groups, by reference to the ancestors buried within them.
2023: 
2024: Figure 2.7 Renfrew’s megaliths on Rousay, Orkney Islands, showing
2025: ‘distribution of chambered tombs in relation to modern arable land
2026: (stippled), with hypothetical territorial boundaries’ (Renfrew 1973a)
2027: 
2028: [c02_image007.jpg]
2029: 
2030: So Renfrew’s arguments:
2031: 
2032: 1 explained rather than merely described the existence and distribution
2033: of megaliths;
2034: 
2035: 2 used ethnographic analogies from societies assumed to be at a similar
2036: level of social development to strengthen his argument – monumental
2037: structures in Polynesia, linked to particular descent groups in
2038: competition over land;
2039: 
2040: 3 stressed environmental factors and hence adaptation to the environment
2041: – the shortage of land;
2042: 
2043: 4 saw megaliths not as one diagnostic trait to define cultures, but as
2044: one functional element of a total cultural system;
2045: 
2046: 5 ‘tested’ his model – by drawing hypothetical divisions between
2047: megaliths on the Scottish island of Orkney, Renfrew claimed to
2048: demonstrate that the megaliths were spaced at the centre of possible
2049: territories, each territory being of approximately equal size (figure
2050: 2.7).
2051: 
2052: Conclusion
2053: 
2054: If the New Archaeology was a revolution, it suffered the same
2055: fundamental problems of any revolution. New Archaeology had a double
2056: slogan: science and anthropology. But like many revolutionary slogans,
2057: discord and disagreement broke out when archaeologists tried to work out
2058: in practice what those slogans really meant. In many ways, the ongoing
2059: story of archaeological theory to the present day can be seen as the
2060: attempt to deepen our understanding of these slogans, and to ‘roll out’
2061: the concerns of New Archaeology beyond their original applications.
2062: 
2063: The next three chapters will deal with these questions and bring the
2064: reader into the present. Chapter 3 will ask: What do we really mean by
2065: the term ‘science’? Chapter 4 will ask: What do we really mean by the
2066: word ‘testing’ how do we evaluate archaeological arguments? Chapter 5
2067: will ask: How do societies work, what do we really mean by
2068: ‘anthropology’?
2069: 
2070: We shall see in the process that debates within archaeological theory
2071: mirror debates in the human sciences such as history, sociology,
2072: cultural anthropology, politics and economics very closely. Questions of
2073: science, testing and how societies really work are central to the human
2074: sciences as a whole.
2075: 
2076: 3
2077:
